mnemonics.title: CIA Triad
mnemonics.content: CIA = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability. Remember: 'Confidential Information Always' - the three pillars of information security that must be protected.

mnemonics.title: Types of Attackers
mnemonics.content: HACK = Hackers, Attackers, Crackers, Kiddies. Remember: 'Hackers And Crackers Know Different Yields' - from script kiddies to advanced persistent threats.

mnemonics.title: Attack Lifecycle
mnemonics.content: KILL = Kill chain phases: Kill, Infiltrate, Locate, exfiltrate. Actually: RECON → WEAPONIZE → DELIVERY → EXPLOITATION → INSTALLATION → COMMAND & CONTROL → ACTIONS ON OBJECTIVES. Remember: 'Rangers Wind Eagles Dive Enormously In Costly Aerial Engagements'.

mnemonics.title: Malware Types
mnemonics.content: VIRUS = Various Infectious Reproductive Organisms eXecutable. Remember: 'Viruses Infect, Replicate, Overwhelm Systems' - needs host file to spread.

mnemonics.title: Social Engineering
mnemonics.content: PHISH = Pretexting, Human interaction, Influence, Social manipulation, Hacking. Remember: 'People Have Inherited Susceptible Humanness' - manipulation of human psychology.

mnemonics.title: Security Controls
mnemonics.content: DIP = Detective, Indicative, Preventive. Actually: ADMINISTRATIVE + TECHNICAL + PHYSICAL. Remember: 'All Digital Technology Needs Expert Management'.

mnemonics.title: OWASP Top 10
mnemonics.content: A1 A2 A3 A4 A5 A6 A7 A8 A9 A10 = Injection, Broken Auth, Sensitive Data Exposure, XML External Entities, Broken Access Control, Security Misconfiguration, XSS, Insecure Deserialization, Using Components with Known Vulnerabilities, Insufficient Logging. Remember: 'I Breached Sensitive XML Files By Cross-Domain Java Under Server Issues'.

acronyms.usage: Core security triad - used when designing any security system to ensure all three aspects are addressed
Used when multiple compromised systems attack a single target, causing service unavailability
Used when an attacker secretly intercepts communication between two parties
Used when malicious SQL code is inserted into queries through user input fields
Used when attackers inject malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users
Used when describing long-term, targeted attacks by sophisticated attackers (often state-sponsored)
Used when malware provides attackers remote control over compromised systems

acronyms.expansion: Intrusion Detection System / Intrusion Prevention System
acronyms.usage: Used when discussing network security monitoring and blocking tools
Used when discussing secure, encrypted network connections over public networks
Used when describing additional authentication layers beyond passwords

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a castle with multiple layers of protection: moat → outer wall → inner wall → guards → keep. Each layer provides security even if the outer layer is breached. Visualize concentric circles or onion layers.

Picture a pyramid: at the top = nation states (small number, high capability), middle = organized crime/APT, bottom = script kiddies (large number, low capability). Height = sophistication, base = number of actors.
Imagine a virus spreading like a biological virus in a crowded room - one person touches another, who touches others, creating a chain reaction. Each infected person can infect multiple others.
Picture fishing with a baited hook. The attacker throws out many hooks (emails) hoping someone bites (falls for the scam). Spear phishing = targeted fishing with specific bait for a specific person.
Imagine a security checkpoint at a building entrance - everyone and everything must pass through and be checked before entering. The firewall is the gatekeeper between trusted and untrusted networks.
Picture a secret door in a building that no one knows about except the attacker. When discovered (day zero), the attacker can walk right in before anyone knows to close it.

analogies.analogy: Like the immune system in your body. Just as your immune system identifies and fights pathogens, antivirus scans for and removes malicious software. Both need regular updates to recognize new threats.

Like a security guard at the entrance of a building. It checks everyone (network traffic) coming in and going out, allowing only authorized people (legitimate traffic) to pass and blocking suspicious individuals (malicious traffic).
Like a key to your house. Just as you wouldn't give your house key to strangers, don't share passwords. A strong password is like a complex lock that's harder to pick.
Like a safe deposit box. Even if someone steals the box (intercepts the data), they can't access the contents without the combination (decryption key).
Like a scam phone call where someone pretends to be from your bank. The attacker pretends to be legitimate (email) to trick you into revealing sensitive information.
Like a mob blocking the entrance to a store, preventing legitimate customers from entering. The overwhelming crowd (traffic) makes the service unavailable to real users.
Like a con artist tricking someone into giving up sensitive information. Instead of technical hacking, attackers manipulate human psychology - like a liar who makes you trust them.
Like a bank heist. Just as robbers break into a bank's vault, attackers break into systems to steal valuable data. Both require security measures and both can have devastating consequences.
